title: Boeuf bourguignon
subtitle: Beef braised in Burgundy wine with lardons, mushrooms, and pearl onions
excerpt: Boeuf bourguignon is a Burgundian braise: braising beef cooked low and slow in red wine until it collapses into something rich and deeply savoury. The lardons and mushrooms added towards the end give the sauce body and texture. It is weekend cooking, long-haul, hands-off, and worth every hour.
sourceNotes:
• Auguste Escoffier, *Le Guide Culinaire* (1903, public domain). Principal reference for the classic Burgundian braise technique, including the marinade option, the browning sequence, and the role of the bouquet garni.
• Julia Child, *Mastering the Art of French Cooking* (1961). The method here follows Child's widely cited approach of browning beef in batches and finishing with mushrooms and onions separately, the version that introduced the dish to English-speaking home cooks.

content.text: is a braise, which means the beef needs time rather than attention. The work comes in the first 30 minutes, browning the meat in batches until deeply coloured, building the base with lardons and vegetables, deglazing with wine. After that, it goes in the oven and you leave it alone for two and a half hours.

Use a full bottle of wine and a wine you would drink. The dish has nowhere to hide a thin, acidic bottle, the wine is the cooking liquid and becomes the sauce. Côtes du Rhône, Merlot, or any medium-bodied red works. Actual Burgundy is not required.
Serve with mashed potato, egg noodles, or crusty bread to catch the sauce. The dish only improves the next day.

Preheat the oven to 160°C (fan 140°C / gas 3). Heat a large casserole over a medium heat. Add {{lardons}} of lardons and cook for 5 minutes until the fat renders and the edges colour. Remove with a slotted spoon and set aside.

Pat {{beef-chuck}} of beef chunks dry with kitchen paper and season well. Working in batches with space between each piece, brown the beef in the lardon fat for 2 to 3 minutes per side until deeply coloured on all surfaces. Remove each batch and set aside. Crowding the pan steams rather than browns.

Add {{olive-oil}} of olive oil to the casserole. Cook {{onion}} chopped onion and {{carrot}} chopped carrot over a medium heat for 8 minutes until softened. Add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes. Sprinkle over {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir for 1 minute.
Return the beef and lardons to the casserole. Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Add the
of {{bay-leaves}} bay leaves and {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme. Bring to a simmer, then cover and transfer to the oven. Cook for 2 hours 30 minutes.

Fry {{mushrooms-chestnut}} of mushrooms in {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a frying pan over a high heat for 4 minutes until golden. Add to the casserole for the final 30 minutes of braising. Remove the herbs, taste, and adjust the seasoning before serving.

items.fix: The oven should be low, the liquid should barely tremble. A hard simmer toughens braising meat. Chuck or shin are the cuts for this dish; lean cuts will not tenderise in the same way.
items.cause: The braise was at too high a temperature, or the beef was not the right cut.
items.symptom: Beef is tough after the full cooking time

items.fix: If the sauce is thin at the end, remove the beef and reduce the liquid on the hob over a medium-high heat for 10 to 15 minutes until it coats a spoon. Return the beef to warm through.
items.cause: The flour was insufficient to thicken the liquid, or the casserole was not covered tightly.

items.fix: Always pat meat dry before browning. Brown in batches with space between each piece. If the pan looks crowded, move to a frying pan and brown in stages.
items.cause: The pan was overcrowded, or the beef was wet.
items.symptom: Beef did not brown. It steamed in the pan

items.fix: A long braise should mellow the wine. If it's still sharp at the end, a small knob of butter stirred in off the heat rounds the flavour. Avoid wines labelled 'cooking wine'. They are often higher in acid and salt.
items.cause: The wine was not reduced enough during the braise, or the bottle was particularly acidic.

content.text: Boeuf bourguignon comes from Burgundy, the region in eastern France known for beef cattle and red wine. The braise is the natural use for both. It became a fixed classic of French cooking in the early twentieth century. An English-language version in the 1960s brought it into American and British home kitchens. It has stayed on the table ever since. The method is sound and the result is hard to argue with.
